passage:
A student is writing a paper about the environmental impact of fast fashion. The student has taken the following notes:
- The fashion industry produces approximately 10% of global carbon emissions—more than aviation and shipping combined.
- Fast fashion companies produce roughly twice as many garments today as they did in 2000.
- The average garment is worn only 7–10 times before being discarded.
- The fashion industry is the second-largest consumer of water globally, requiring about 2,700 liters of water to produce a single cotton T-shirt.
- Textile dyeing and treatment accounts for approximately 20% of global industrial water pollution.
question: Which choice most effectively uses the notes to characterize the fashion industry's environmental footprint?

choices.A: Fast fashion has grown rapidly, and the fashion industry uses a lot of water.
choices.B: The fashion industry generates approximately 10% of global carbon emissions—exceeding aviation and shipping combined—while consuming vast quantities of water (2,700 liters per T-shirt) and contributing roughly 20% of global industrial water pollution.
choices.C: The fashion industry pollutes the environment in various ways, which is why many consumers are switching to sustainable brands.
choices.D: Because garments are worn only 7–10 times on average, the fashion industry produces far more clothing than necessary.

explanation: B synthesizes three distinct environmental dimensions—carbon emissions (with the aviation/shipping comparison), water consumption (with the per-T-shirt figure), and water pollution (the 20% share)—into a comprehensive characterization of the environmental footprint. A is vague, mentioning only growth and water use without quantifying either. C introduces 'sustainable brands' as context not found in the notes and provides no specific data. D focuses only on the overproduction/underuse pattern and does not address carbon or water impacts, which are central to the question's goal.
